Function: nnp_build_cell_list_cache Module: cp2k.psmp Source: nnp_acsf.F:2166-2329 [...] Coverage (incl. loops): 0.08% | (excl. loops): 0.01% 
/home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F: 2166 - 2329
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


2166:    SUBROUTINE nnp_build_cell_list_cache(nnp, cell, exact_cutoff, list_cutoff, skin)


[...]


2179:       CALL timeset(routineN, handle)


2180: 


2181:       ASSOCIATE (cl => cell_list_cache)


2182: 


2183:          ! Snapshot rebuild-trigger state and reference coords


2184:          cl%num_atoms = nnp%num_atoms


2185:          cl%exact_cutoff = exact_cutoff


2186:          cl%list_cutoff = list_cutoff


2187:          cl%verlet_skin = skin


2188:          cl%perd = cell%perd


2189:          cl%hmat = cell%hmat


2190:          cl%h_inv = cell%h_inv


2191:          cl%orthorhombic = cell%orthorhombic


2192:          cl%ref_coord_primary(:, :) = cl%coord_primary(:, :)


2193: 


2194:          ! Number of integer PBC shifts per axis that span list_cutoff.


2195:          CALL nnp_compute_pbc_copies(cl%image_copies, cell, list_cutoff)


[...]


2204:          pmin(:) = HUGE(1.0_dp)


2205:          pmax(:) = -HUGE(1.0_dp)


2206:          DO j = 1, nnp%num_atoms


2207:             DO d = 1, 3


2208:                IF (cl%coord_primary(d, j) < pmin(d)) pmin(d) = cl%coord_primary(d, j)


2209:                IF (cl%coord_primary(d, j) > pmax(d)) pmax(d) = cl%coord_primary(d, j)


2210:             END DO


2211:          END DO


2212:          wlow(:) = pmin(:) - (list_cutoff + rebuild_eps)


2213:          whigh(:) = pmax(:) + (list_cutoff + rebuild_eps)


2214: 


2215:          ! Pass 1: count the images inside the halo window.


2216:          n_kept = 0


2217:          DO tz = -cl%image_copies(3), cl%image_copies(3)


2218:          DO ty = -cl%image_copies(2), cl%image_copies(2)


2219:          DO tx = -cl%image_copies(1), cl%image_copies(1)


2220:             trans(:) = cell%hmat(:, 1)*REAL(tx, dp) + &


2221:                        cell%hmat(:, 2)*REAL(ty, dp) + &


2222:                        cell%hmat(:, 3)*REAL(tz, dp)


2223:             DO j = 1, nnp%num_atoms


2224:                ipos(:) = cl%coord_primary(:, j) + trans(:)


2225:                IF (ipos(1) < wlow(1) .OR. ipos(1) > whigh(1)) CYCLE


2226:                IF (ipos(2) < wlow(2) .OR. ipos(2) > whigh(2)) CYCLE


2227:                IF (ipos(3) < wlow(3) .OR. ipos(3) > whigh(3)) CYCLE


2228:                n_kept = n_kept + 1


2229:             END DO


2230:          END DO


2231:          END DO


2232:          END DO


2233: 


2234:          n_img = n_kept


2235:          cl%n_images = n_img


2236: 


2237:          IF (ALLOCATED(cl%image_atom)) DEALLOCATE (cl%image_atom)


2238:          IF (ALLOCATED(cl%image_shift)) DEALLOCATE (cl%image_shift)


2239:          IF (ALLOCATED(cl%image_translation)) DEALLOCATE (cl%image_translation)


2240:          ALLOCATE (cl%image_atom(n_img))


2241:          ALLOCATE (cl%image_shift(3, n_img))


2242:          ALLOCATE (cl%image_translation(3, n_img))


2243: 


2244:          ! Pass 2: fill the image table and the bounding box of kept images.


2245:          cl%lower(:) = HUGE(1.0_dp)


2246:          cl%upper(:) = -HUGE(1.0_dp)


2247:          img = 0


2248:          DO tz = -cl%image_copies(3), cl%image_copies(3)


2249:          DO ty = -cl%image_copies(2), cl%image_copies(2)


2250:          DO tx = -cl%image_copies(1), cl%image_copies(1)


2251:             trans(:) = cell%hmat(:, 1)*REAL(tx, dp) + &


2252:                        cell%hmat(:, 2)*REAL(ty, dp) + &


2253:                        cell%hmat(:, 3)*REAL(tz, dp)


2254:             DO j = 1, nnp%num_atoms


2255:                ipos(:) = cl%coord_primary(:, j) + trans(:)


2256:                IF (ipos(1) < wlow(1) .OR. ipos(1) > whigh(1)) CYCLE


2257:                IF (ipos(2) < wlow(2) .OR. ipos(2) > whigh(2)) CYCLE


2258:                IF (ipos(3) < wlow(3) .OR. ipos(3) > whigh(3)) CYCLE


2259:                img = img + 1


2260:                cl%image_atom(img) = j


2261:                cl%image_shift(1, img) = tx


2262:                cl%image_shift(2, img) = ty


2263:                cl%image_shift(3, img) = tz


2264:                cl%image_translation(:, img) = trans(:)


2265:                DO d = 1, 3


2266:                   IF (ipos(d) < cl%lower(d)) cl%lower(d) = ipos(d)


2267:                   IF (ipos(d) > cl%upper(d)) cl%upper(d) = ipos(d)


2268:                END DO


2269:             END DO


2270:          END DO


2271:          END DO


2272:          END DO


2273: 


2274:          ! Pad the bounding box by half a skin so atoms can drift before rebuild.


2275:          cl%lower(:) = cl%lower(:) - (0.5_dp*skin + rebuild_eps)


2276:          cl%upper(:) = cl%upper(:) + (0.5_dp*skin + rebuild_eps)


2277: 


2278:          ! Coarse grid: bin_width ~ list_cutoff so bin_span is 1 (3x3x3 walk).


2279:          DO d = 1, 3


2280:             cl%nbin(d) = MAX(1, CEILING((cl%upper(d) - cl%lower(d))/list_cutoff))


2281:             cl%bin_width(d) = (cl%upper(d) - cl%lower(d))/REAL(cl%nbin(d), dp)


2282:             IF (cl%nbin(d) == 1) THEN


2283:                cl%bin_span(d) = 0


2284:             ELSE


2285:                cl%bin_span(d) = MIN(cl%nbin(d) - 1, CEILING(list_cutoff/cl%bin_width(d)))


2286:                IF (cl%bin_span(d) < 1) cl%bin_span(d) = 1


2287:             END IF


2288:          END DO


2289: 


2290:          n_cells_total = cl%nbin(1)*cl%nbin(2)*cl%nbin(3)


2291:          cl%n_cells = n_cells_total


2292: 


2293:          IF (ALLOCATED(cl%head)) DEALLOCATE (cl%head)


2294:          IF (ALLOCATED(cl%next)) DEALLOCATE (cl%next)


2295:          ALLOCATE (cl%head(n_cells_total))


2296:          ALLOCATE (cl%next(n_img))


2297:          cl%head(:) = -1


2298:          cl%next(:) = -1


2299: 


2300:          ! Link each image into its bin's chain (LIFO at the head).


2301:          DO img = 1, n_img


2302:             j = cl%image_atom(img)


2303:             ipos(:) = cl%coord_primary(:, j) + cl%image_translation(:, img)


2304:             CALL nnp_cell_list_bin_from_position(ipos, bin)


2305:             cell_idx = nnp_cell_list_linear_index(bin(1), bin(2), bin(3))


2306:             cl%next(img) = cl%head(cell_idx)


2307:             cl%head(cell_idx) = img


2308:          END DO


2309: 


2310:          ! Longest chain; chains are static until the next rebuild, so this


2311:          ! exactly bounds what any 3x3x3 (or wider) walk can visit per bin.


2312:          cl%max_bin_occupancy = 0


2313:          DO cell_idx = 1, n_cells_total


2314:             d = 0


2315:             img = cl%head(cell_idx)


2316:             DO WHILE (img > 0)


2317:                d = d + 1


2318:                img = cl%next(img)


2319:             END DO


2320:             IF (d > cl%max_bin_occupancy) cl%max_bin_occupancy = d


2321:          END DO


2322: 


2323:          cl%initialized = .TRUE.


2324: 


2325:       END ASSOCIATE


2326: 


2327:       CALL timestop(handle)


2328: 


2329:    END SUBROUTINE nnp_build_cell_list_cache

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 235.40 to 49.80 cycles (4.73x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is not vectorized.
Only 13% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).


Details


11% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 14% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 4% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 33% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX divide and square root instructions are used in vector version.
  • 22% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.


Workaround


Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


512-bits vectorization


On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.


Workaround


Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Masked instructions


Detected masked instructions.

Details


Vector registers are partially exploited, which is expected if your function is irregular or mixes elements of different sizes.

Workaround


If your function is irregular, try to remove or hoist conditional structures out of your function. If it mixes elements of different sizes, try to uniformize them.

FMA


Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

Workaround


  • Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:

    • use axHost or xHost
  • Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).



  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.

These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

Avoid mixing data with different types. In particular, check if the type of constants is the same as array elements.

18 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).
4 AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in vector mode (four at a time).


Matching between your function (in the source code) and the binary function


The binary function is composed of 34 FP arithmetical operations:

  • 9: divide
The binary function is loading 1806 bytes (225 double precision FP elements).
The binary function is storing 1344 bytes (168 double precision FP elements).

Cycles and memory resources usage


Detected masked instructions: assuming all mask elements are active.
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 235.40 cycles. At this rate:

  • 5% of peak load performance is reached (7.67 out of 128.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 8% of peak store performance is reached (5.71 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 235.40 to 179.00 cycles (1.32x speedup).

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 235.40 to 49.80 cycles (4.73x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is not vectorized.
Only 13% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).


Details


11% of SSE/AVX instructions are used in vector version (process two or more data elements in vector registers):

  • 14% of SSE/AVX loads are used in vector version.
  • 4% of SSE/AVX stores are used in vector version.
  • 33% of SSE/AVX addition or subtraction instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX multiply instructions are used in vector version.
  • 0% of SSE/AVX divide and square root instructions are used in vector version.
  • 22% of SSE/AVX instructions that are not load, store, addition, subtraction nor multiply instructions are used in vector version.


Workaround


Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


512-bits vectorization


On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.


Workaround


Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high

Masked instructions


Detected masked instructions.

Details


Vector registers are partially exploited, which is expected if your function is irregular or mixes elements of different sizes.

Workaround


If your function is irregular, try to remove or hoist conditional structures out of your function. If it mixes elements of different sizes, try to uniformize them.

FMA


Presence of both ADD/SUB and MUL operations.

Workaround


  • Pass to your compiler a micro-architecture specialization option:

    • use axHost or xHost
  • Try to change order in which elements are evaluated (using parentheses) in arithmetic expressions containing both ADD/SUB and MUL operations to enable your compiler to generate FMA instructions wherever possible.
For instance a + b*c is a valid FMA (MUL then ADD).
However (a+b)* c cannot be translated into an FMA (ADD then MUL).



  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Cycles and memory resources usage


Detected masked instructions: assuming all mask elements are active.
Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 235.40 cycles. At this rate:

  • 5% of peak load performance is reached (7.67 out of 128.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 8% of peak store performance is reached (5.71 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))


Front-end bottlenecks


Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

By removing all these bottlenecks, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 235.40 to 179.00 cycles (1.32x speedup).
